summary: Gruber responds to accusations of hypocrisy for defending Apple's 'Hello' iPhone commercial against charges it ripped off Christian Marclay's 1995 film Telephones, while simultaneously criticizing LogoMaid for copying SimpleBits' logo. He argues that rip-offs exist on a continuum rather than as a binary judgment, and draws a distinction between copying an idea versus copying a specific implementation. He classifies several Apple rip-off accusations (Dashboard vs. Konfabulator, iPod vs. Lugz, Intel commercial vs. Postal Service video) differently based on this framework. Ultimately, he concedes that even if Apple's commercial isn't technically a rip-off, a company that positions itself as a champion of creativity should hold itself to a higher standard.
key_points: Rip-offs exist on a continuum from wholly original to blatant copy, not as a binary yes/no judgment
The key distinction is whether work derives from the idea of an original or from the original work itself
Apple is hypocritical for mocking Microsoft's copying while appropriating others' ideas, though Vista's features are arguably original implementations of Mac OS X concepts
A company that presents itself as a champion of the creative class should hold its advertising to a higher originality standard than merely 'not a rip-off'
The thought experiment of Jonathan Ive designing for Sony illustrates the murky question of who owns a creative sensibility
money_quotes: Drawing the line between what's a rip-off and what's original, but inspired by something else, can be highly subjective.
To me, it's not a binary decision, yes or no, but instead a continuum, with wholly original works on one side and blatant bit-for-bit copies on the other.
My basic rule of thumb is to gauge whether the work in question is derived from the idea of the original work, or derived from the original work itself.
What if Sony hired Jonathan Ive and he designed for them an iPod-esque Walkman? Is it iPod-ishness or Ive-ness?

It's true that most of the new features in Vista are similar to long-standing features in Mac OS X, but none of them are rip-offs. They strike me as falling under the category of original implementations of the same idea. Lack of innovation, yes, but not theft.
"Not a rip-off" isn't good enough for a company that presents itself as a champion of the creative class.
Rip-off or not, if Marclay didn't want Apple to use the idea, they shouldn't have used it. Not because it was wrong, but because Apple can do better.
key_insight: The distinction between copying an idea and copying its execution is the crux of creative originality, but a company that brands itself as a creative champion should aspire to more than just clearing the 'not a rip-off' bar.
